CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A : Questions number 1 to 5 are very short answer questions and carry 1 mark each.
  3. Section B : Questions number 6 to 12 are short answer questions and carry 2 marks each.
  4. Section C : Questions number 13 to 24 are also short answer questions and carry 3 marks each.
  5. Section D : Questions number 25 to 27 are long answer questions and carry 5 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions of one mark, two questions of two marks, four questions of three marks and all the three questions of five marks weightage. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
  7. Use of log tables, if necessary. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Out of KCl and AgCl, which one shows Schottky defect and why?

Solution:

Chapter deleted from the textbook.

 1

OR

Why does ZnO appear yellow on heating?

Solution:

ZnO is white at room temperature. ZnO loses oxygen on heating. The excess of Zn2+ ions moves to interstitial sites and vacant sites of anions are occupied by electrons which absorb light from visible region and radiate complementary colour i.e yellow.

{tex}ZnO\xrightarrow\triangle\;Zn^{2+}\;+\frac12O_2\;+2e^-{/tex}

 1
2.

Arrange the following in decreasing order of basic character:
C6H5NH2, (CH3)3N, C2H5NH2

Solution:

Decreasing order of basic character is:
(CH3)3N > C2H5NH2 > C6H5NH2

 1
3.

What type of colloid is formed when a solid is dispersed in a liquid? Give an example.

Solution:

Sol is formed when a solid is dispersed in a liquid
Example: Paints, gels.

 1
4.

Out of Chlorobenzene and Cyclohexyl chloride, which one is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction and why?

Solution:

Cyclohexyl chloride; Because of partial double bond character of C-Cl bond in Chlorobenzene, Resonance effect and sp3 hybridised carbon in cyclohexyl chloride whereas sp2 carbon in chlorobenzene.

 1
5.

What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?

Solution:

Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble).
Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C1 of one glucose unit and C4 of the next glucose unit.

 1

OR

Write the products obtained after hydrolysis of DNA.

Solution:

Hydrolysis of DNA gives 2-deoxyribose, nitrogen containing heterocyclic base( Adenine, Guanine, Cytosine and Thymine), phosphoric acid.

 1

SECTION B
6.

Write balanced chemical equations for the following processes:

  1. Cl2 is passed through slaked lime.
  2. SO2 gas is passed through an aqueous solution of Fe(III) salt.
Solution:
  1. {tex}2Ca(OH)_2 + 2Cl_2 \rightarrow CaCl_2 + Ca(OCl)_2 + 2H_2O{/tex}
  2. {tex}SO_2 + 2Fe^{3+} + 2H_2O  \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+{/tex}
 2

OR
  1. Write two poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Why does Cu2+ solution give blue colour on reaction with ammonia?
Solution:
  1. Mustard gas, tear gas, phosgene are poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Because it forms blue coloured complex [Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
    Reaction: Cu2+ + 4NH3 (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}[Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
 2
7.

Give reasons:

  1. Cooking is faster in pressure cooker than in cooking pan.
  2. Red Blood Cells (RBC) shrink when placed in saline water but swell in distilled water.
Solution:
  1. Due to increase of pressure in cooker, boiling point of water increases. So, cooking is faster in pressure cooker than in cooking pan.
  2. Red Blood Cells (RBC) shrink when placed in saline water but swell in distilled water because Red Blood Cells loose water in saline water and absorb water in distilled water due to osmosis.
 2
8.

Define order of reaction. Predict the order of reaction in the given graphs:




  2. where [R]o is the initial concentration of reactant and t1/2 is half-life.
Solution:

Order of reaction: The order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the concentration of the reactants in the rate equation.

  1. First order
  2. zero order
 2
9.

When FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A). Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B). Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C). An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D). Identify (A), (B), (C) and (D).

Solution:

FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A).

Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B).

Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C).

An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D).


Therefore,
A = Na2CrO4 = Sodium chromate,
B= Na2Cr2O7 = Sodium dichromate,
C= K2Cr2O7 = Potassium dichromate,
D= Na2SO4 = Sodium sulfate

 2
10.

Write IUPAC name of the complex [Co(en)2(NO2)Cl]+. What type of structural isomerism is shown by this complex?

Solution:

IUPAC name of the complex [Co(en)2(NO2)Cl]+ is Chloro bis ethane-1, 2-diamine nitrito-N-cobalt(III) ion.
Linkage isomerism is shown by this complex.
Linkage isomers are [Co(en)2(NO2)Cl]+ and [Co(en)2 (ONO)Cl]+.

 2

OR

Using IUPAC norms, write the formulae for the following complexes:

  1. Hexaaquachromium(III) chloride
  2. Sodium trioxalatoferrate(III)
Solution:
  1. Formula of Hexaaquachromium(III) chloride is [Cr(H2O)6]Cl3
  2. Formula of Sodium trioxalatoferrate(III) is Na3[Fe(ox)3]
 2
11.
    Although both [NiCl4]2- and [Ni(CO)4] have sp3 hybridisation yet [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic and [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic. Give reason. (Atomic no. of Ni = 28)
  2. Write the electronic configuration of d5 on the basis of crystal field theory when
    1. {tex}\Delta {/tex}0 < P and
    2. {tex}\Delta {/tex}0 > P
Solution:
  1. In [NiCl4]2-, Cl- is a weak field ligand due to which there are two unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.
    In [Ni(CN)4]2-, CN- is a strong field ligand, it leads to pairing of electrons in 3d- orbital. So there are no unpaired electron in 3d- orbital.
    Hence, [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic and [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic in nature.
  2. The electronic configuration of d5 on the basis of crystal field theory is 
    1. t2g3 eg2
    2. t2g5eg0
 2

17.

Write the name and principle of the method used for refining of (a) Zinc, (b) Germanium, (c) Titanium.

Solution:
  1. Distillation: This is very useful for low boiling metals like zinc and mercury.
    The impure metal is evaporated to obtain the pure metal as distillate. The more basic metal remains in the solution and the less basic metal goes to the anode mud.
  2. Zone refining: This method is based on the principle that the impurities are more soluble in the melt than in the solid-state of the metal.
    A circular mobile heater is fixed at one end of a rod of the impure metal. The molten zone moves along with the heater which is moved forward. As the heater moves forward, the pure metal crystallizes out and the impurities pass on into the adjacent molten zone. The process is repeated several times and the heater is moved in the same direction. At one end, impurities get concentrated. This end is cut off.
  3. Van Arkel method: This method is very useful for removing all the oxygen and nitrogen present in the form of impurity in ;metals.
    The crude metal is heated in an evacuated vessel with iodine. The metal iodide being more covalent, volatilizes.
    Ti + 2I2 → TiI4
    The metal iodide is decomposed on a tungsten filament, electrically heated to about 1800K. The pure metal is thus deposited on the filament.
    TiI4 → Ti + 2I2

Write the name and principle of the method used for refining of (a) Zinc, (b) Germanium, (c) Titanium. Solution: Distillation: This is very useful for low boiling metals like zinc and mercury. The impure metal is evaporated to obtain the pure metal as distillate. The more basic metal remains in the solution and the less basic metal goes to the anode mud. Zone refining: This method is based on the principle that the impurities are more soluble in the melt than in the solid-state of the metal. A circular mobile heater is fixed at one end of a rod of the impure metal. The molten zone moves along with the heater which is moved forward. As the heater moves forward, the pure metal crystallizes out and the impurities pass on into the adjacent molten zone. The process is repeated several times and the heater is moved in the same direction. At one end, impurities get concentrated. This end is cut off. Van Arkel method: This method is very useful for removing all the oxygen and nitrogen present in the form of impurity in ;metals. The crude metal is heated in an evacuated vessel with iodine. The metal iodide being more covalent, volatilizes. Ti + 2I 2 → TiI 4 The metal iodide is decomposed on a tungsten filament, electrically heated to about 1800K. The pure metal is thus deposited on the filament. TiI 4 → Ti + 2I 2 3 18. Give reasons for the following: Transition metals form complex compounds. E o values for (Zn 2+ /Zn) and (Mn 2 +/Mn) are more negative than expected due to stable 3d 10 configuration in Zn 2+ and 3d 5 configuration in Mn 2+ . Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d and 7s orbitals. 3 19.
